data.narrative: Built API-key authentication for the admin surface, kept the redirect endpoint public, and added owner-scoped link access plus rate limiting. Diagnosed the injected auth incident by separating authentication from authorization and tracing the likely fault to identity handoff during link creation, then closed the loop with a safer config pattern and regression check.

dimension_scores.observation: Chose API keys over JWT because the app is an internal/admin surface rather than a full user-login product.
dimension_scores.evidence: Explained that API keys fit the limited /links surface without adding login-flow complexity, while JWT would make more sense for real user accounts and roles.

dimension_scores.observation: Used a hypothesis-driven incident flow to separate auth failure from owner-scoping failure before naming the likely root cause.
dimension_scores.evidence: Explained that 401 on unauthenticated /links proves auth is active, while 404 on authenticated reads points to authorization lookup and likely wrong created_by storage.

dimension_scores.observation: Recognized that auth alone is insufficient and tied the design to ownership enforcement, key rotation, and public-vs-protected route boundaries.
dimension_scores.evidence: Said that ownership enforcement must accompany auth and that leaked keys should be revoked or rotated in config or key storage.

dimension_scores.observation: Verified the protected/public split and rate limiting with concrete runtime behavior instead of stopping at code changes.
dimension_scores.evidence: Verified /links without auth returned 401, same-owner reads succeeded, cross-owner reads returned 404, and repeated create-link and redirect requests eventually returned 429.

dimension_scores.observation: Explained the threat model and auth flow in plain language, including why /r/<code> remains public.
dimension_scores.evidence: Described GET /r/<code> as the actual shared product surface, while /links routes create and expose stored data and therefore require protection.

strengths: Clear separation between authentication and authorization during the incident diagnosis.
Good production instinct on hiding resource existence with 404 for cross-owner access.
Grounded verification in real request outcomes instead of relying on code inspection alone.
data.recommendation: Carry the same identity-to-ownership thinking into Module 05 by defining error handling that is safe for attackers but still useful for operators.

public_evidence.artifact: Protected admin API and public redirect surface
public_evidence.student_action: Implemented API-key auth for /links, enforced owner-scoped link access, and verified the public/protected split with live requests.
public_evidence.technical_detail: X-API-Key dependency for /links, owner filtering through principal_id/created_by, and rate limits for create-link and redirect traffic.
public_evidence.proof: GET /links without auth returned 401; POST /links with API_KEY_A returned 201; GET /links/{id} with API_KEY_B for A's link returned 404; repeated create-link and redirect requests returned 429 after the configured threshold.
public_evidence.ability: Builds and verifies authenticated API behavior with scoped authorization.
public_evidence.reviewer_value: Shows the student can turn a security requirement into working route protection, ownership enforcement, and abuse controls.

concepts_demonstrated.evidence: Explained that X-API-Key maps to principal_id for identity, while owner-scoped queries decide what that principal can access.
Used the same-key 200 / different-key 404 check as end-to-end proof of ownership enforcement.
Explained that GET /r/<code> must stay public because it is the customer-facing product path.

teaching_approaches.detail: Used the actual /links and /r/<code> flows plus the 401/404 split to scaffold authentication versus authorization.
